content.full_text:
Q3-245

OHLONE FAMILIES CONSULTING SERVICES
1845 THE ALAMEDA, SAN JOSE CA. 95126 TEL. 408-293-9956 FAX 293-7843

ELDERS:
ENOS SANCHEZ
ROBERT SANCHEZ
DOLORES SANCHEZ
MARGRET S. MARTINEZ

April 14, 1992

Louisa Menchaca
Legal Division of
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Ste. 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Menchaca,

Per our telephone conversation on April 9, 1992 regarding a City
of San Jose - Memorandum, as it relates to the Political Reform
Act: Consultant conflicts of interest.

The underlying facts are as follows:

1. Ohlone Families Consulting Services is a private firm
DBA, partnership owned by Rosemary Cambra, President and
Norma Sanchez, Vice President.

2. OFCS was contracted to do consulting work on September
1991, with the specified language that OFCS would support Redevelopment Agency in the research design, development and administration of Agency policy concerning
the treatment of Native American burial sites. It should
be noted here that San Jose City Council sits as Redevelopment Agency Board.

3. Rosemary Cambra, also represents her tribe as chairwoman
of the Muwekma Ohlone Tribe. As Chairwoman and
spokesperson for her tribe, she has been asked by the
tribes councilmembers to address the Native American
Burial issue as it relates to the disposition of their
ancestral dead. Therefore, Rosemary Cambra wrote the
following letters in 1991:

a) March 1991 to Susan Hammer, Mayor for the City of San
Jose, regarding new federal law that would address
the Native American Burial issue. We were then
directed to meet with Kent Dewell, Deputy City Manager.

b) On May 1, 1991, we met with Kent Dewell, and Rudy
Navarro to discuss the new federal law and the
possibility of developing a Native American Burial
Policy.

c) On June 17, 1991, Rosemary wrote a letter to Kent
Dewell, thanking him for the meeting and recapping
on our discussion at that meeting.

Page 2

d) On November 23, 1991, a letter went out to Council woman Pat Sausedo regarding a specific development where Native American Remains were encountered in her District. The concerns at that time were that SB297 and SB447, State Laws were not followed, due to the fact that Rosemary Cambra, as Most Likely Descendant under the Native American Heritage Commissions List was not contacted nor were other MLD's, making this a violation of the law. Since this type of incident has had a history of repeating itself in San Jose, Rosemary asked for a meeting as Chairwoman of the Muwekma Ohlone Tribe, with other Muwekma Elders and representatives to discuss a development of an ordinance.

e) Councilwoman Pat Sausedo felt that this issue was sensitive enough to address, being that their are state and federal laws in place to consider the development of an ordinance applicable to both City and Redevelopment, that will address Native American Burials. The Muwekma Ohlone Tribe will be recommending that the City consider adoption of an ordinance tailored after Native American Graves and Protection and Repatriation Act, Public Law 101-601, 25USC 3002, where the disposition on ancestral remains lies in the jurisdiction of the aboriginal local tribes and that would include language to make it applicable to both public, and private developments.

f) Therefore, a Native American Burial Ad Hoc Committee was developed under Councilwoman Pat Sausedo's Office, with representatives from the Mayors office, City Managers Office, Redevelopment Agency's Office, a private developer, and U.S. Justice Department, as well as, Rosemary Cambra, Chairwoman of the Muwekma Ohlone Tribe.

QUESTIONS:

1. Would Rosemary Cambra, as Chairwoman of the Muwekma Ohlone Tribe, and Norma Sanchez, Tribal Administrator be disqualified from participating on Councilwoman Pat Sausedo's Ad Hoc Committee, because of the current contract with the Redevelopment Agency's contract with OFCS? and if so, could the Muwekma Tribe designate another tribal representative to address this issue at this time, since she was representing the philosophical/religious views of the tribal members (approx.250 enrolled members), as it address their ancestral dead.

2. Since we are serving at an advisory capacity as representatives of our community will this p ropose a potential conflict?

Page 3

3. How does this situation differ, for example, from someone serving on the FEMA board, making policy decisions, but can still get paid monies for homeless programs?

4. How does this situation also differ from an archaeologist serving on a commission or burial committee at an advisory capacity for the County and holds existing archaeological consulting contracts with the county?

In conclusion, if my business partner and I are in a potential consultant conflict of interest, please let me know along with some recommendations to resolve this potential dilemma in a professional manner. I nor my business partner wish to be in violation of the Political Reform Act, nor is it our intention to put anyone in an embarrassing position, nor was it our intention to use a public officials position to influence any governmental decision in which one may perceive us having financial interest.

The main and only intent was that professionals providing cultural resource services comply with the laws in place and we felt that by developing an ordinance this would help keep archaeological consultants on track with these laws and would reaffirm the City's intent in enforcing the laws in place by adopting an ordinance that would address the Native American Burial Issue.

As you can see, the Native American Burial Issue is highly complex, however, we are only interested in complying with the laws and practicing fair and ethical professional practices.

Therefore, we will await for your formal opinion on my behalf and if you should have any further questions regarding this matter please do not hesitate to call me or my business partner at (408) 293-9956.

Sincerely,

Rosemary Cambra                    Norma E. Sanchez
OFCS President                     OFCS Vice President

ENCLOSURES

Distribution:
    Vice Mayor Pat Saucedo
    Nabar Martinez
    Richard Rios
    Jorge Gonzales
    Eva Terazas
    Susan Mineta
    Ken Christie
